burial.plot_notes: Old North Cemetery, Hartford CT — buried with husband Frederick Augustus Hale and several children including James Woodbridge Hale, Olive Rockwell Hale, and Edith Stiles Hale.

narrative_blocks.header: Married thirty, lost three of six children
narrative_blocks.body: Julia Stiles married Frederick A Hale of Glastonbury CT on April 7, 1825 at age 30 — late by the era's standards. She bore six children over the next fourteen years; three of them died young (an infant daughter in 1826, James at 10 months in 1830, Alfred at 7 months in 1832). The three who survived to adulthood were Charles Frederick Hale MD (1827, Panama Railroad chief medical officer), James Woodbridge Hale (1833), and Cornelius Stiles Hale (1839, died San Antonio TX 1876 unmarried).

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909), entry 1207
Henry Reed Stiles, The Stiles Family in America (1895), entry 417

burial.plot_notes: Old North Cemetery, Hartford CT — buried with husband Frederick Augustus Hale and several children including James Woodbridge Hale, Olive Rockwell Hale, and Edith Stiles Hale.

She bore six children over the next fourteen years; three of them died young (an infant daughter in 1826, James at 10 months in 1830, Alfred at 7 months in 1832). parents.father_research_notes: Of Glastonbury CT. Not in JSON.
parents.mother_research_notes: Of Glastonbury CT, maiden name Stevens. Not in JSON.

narrative_blocks.header: Infant died young
narrative_blocks.body: Infant died in 1826, in her first year.

education.notes: Hartford, CT. Preparatory education at Hopkins Grammar School in Hartford CT — same school later attended by Sherman Day Thacher (I00592, founder of The Thacher School).
New York City. Entered Oct 1850. Graduated March 1855 with M.D.

career.notes: Approximately 1853. The Panama Railroad — completed 1855 — was the first transcontinental railroad in the Americas and a major engineering project of the era, with high disease mortality among workers.
NYC private practice until c.1859, when he quit partly for impaired health and partly for business interests in West Virginia.

career.organization: Forest Hill Mining Co. (Coal Valley, West Virginia)
career.notes: President of the Forest Hill Mining Co. at Coal Valley WV from 1859-1862. Operations suspended in 1862 due to the Civil War; Forest Hill is in modern Kanawha County WV.

narrative_blocks.header: Panama Railroad Chief Medical Officer about 1853
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Frederick Hale graduated A.M. from Trinity College in Hartford 1851, took his M.D. from the College of Physicians and Surgeons NYC, and around 1853 served as Chief Medical Officer of the Panama Railroad Company — the first transcontinental railroad in the Americas, then under construction across the Isthmus. The position handled the engineering project's notorious tropical-disease mortality among workers.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife died at thirty, six years married
narrative_blocks.body: Charles married Sarah Silvia Sibree (daughter of Robert Sibree of Bath, England) in New York City on June 26, 1853. She died at NYC on September 11, 1859 at age 30 years and 20 days, leaving two young children: Mary Astrea (4) and Charles Brinley (1). His subsequent move to Forest Hill WV in 1859 — President of Forest Hill Mining Co. — coincided with his widowhood.

sources.label: Henry Reed Stiles, The Stiles Family in America (1895), entry 419 with footnote

parents.father_research_notes: Robert Sibree of Bath, England. Not in JSON.

narrative_blocks.header: Greek mythology middle name, virgin goddess of justice
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Astrea Hale's striking middle name 'Astrea' refers to the Greek goddess Astraea — the celestial virgin associated with justice and innocence, who in Ovid's account abandoned earth at the end of the Golden Age and became the constellation Virgo. The classical literary choice fits her father Charles Frederick Hale MD's 1850s educated NYC physician milieu. Her mother Sarah Silvia Sibree (born Bath England) brought literary culture from the English side; Mary was four when her mother died.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at twenty, sixteen months after mother
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Brinley Hale was born May 31, 1858 in New York City and lost his mother Sarah Silvia Sibree Hale only sixteen months later (September 1859). He was raised by his physician father Charles F Hale MD through the family's move from NYC to Forest Hill, West Virginia in 1859, and died November 11, 1878 at age 20, unmarried — twenty years after his birth and nineteen years after losing his mother.

narrative_blocks.header: James died young
narrative_blocks.body: James died in 1830, in his first year. His parents gave the name to a brother born three years later.

narrative_blocks.header: Alfred died young
narrative_blocks.body: Alfred died in 1832, in his first year.

burial.plot_notes: Old North Cemetery, Hartford CT — buried with wife Olive (d. same year, 1921), parents, daughter Edith, and grandparents.

education.notes: Hartford, CT. Hopkins Grammar School (INST001) — same school his older brother Charles Frederick Hale MD (HD4164) attended.

career.notes: Employed for 50 years with Charles Hart Co. of Hartford CT. The Charles Hart Co. was a Hartford merchant/business firm; specific industry not yet documented.

institutions.institution_blurb: Hopkins Grammar School, Hartford CT — same school his older brother Charles Frederick Hale MD attended.

naming_inspiration.description: First name 'James' from his older brother (HD4165) who died at 10 months in July 1830 — three years before James Woodbridge's August 1833 birth. Replacement-naming pattern.

cross_connections.display_label: was his father-in-law; Civil War US Navy commander, USS Hope and

narrative_blocks.header: Fifty years at Charles Hart Company Hartford
narrative_blocks.body: James Woodbridge Hale spent fifty years employed by the Charles Hart Co. of Hartford CT — a half-century at one Hartford firm. He attended Hopkins Grammar School in his youth, the same institution his older brother Dr. Charles Frederick Hale had prepared at decades earlier. He and his wife Olive Post Rockwell Hale were among the oldest members of Hartford's Center Congregational Church.

bio.extra_names: Olive Post (Stiles book rendering, possibly previous-marriage surname or family naming pattern)

burial.plot_notes: Old North Cemetery, Hartford CT — buried with husband James Woodbridge Hale who died 4 months earlier.

parents.father_research_notes: Civil War US Navy officer; see X02822 easter_egg entry.
parents.mother_research_notes: Ruama M. 'Emma' Ayer Rockwell (1822-1847) — died when Olive was 5. Not built per scope (Capt. John's other children/spouse not documented for the Hooker tree).

narrative_blocks.header: She died four months after her husband
narrative_blocks.body: Olive Rockwell married James Woodbridge Hale in 1866 and outlived him by only four months, dying at Hartford in 1921. She had lost her own mother at the age of five.

burial.plot_notes: Old North Cemetery, Hartford CT — buried with parents.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Stiles' carries her father James Woodbridge Hale's mother's maiden surname — Julia Stiles Hale (HD4160). The Stiles name persists into gen 8 of this Hale line.

narrative_blocks.header: Society of Founders of Hartford, never married
narrative_blocks.body: Edith Stiles Hale was a member of the Society of the Descendants of the Founders of Hartford — an institutional recognition of her descent from the founding generation, the same Hooker ancestry that organizes this genealogical project a century later. She never married, lived 75-76 years (1874-1950), and was buried at Old North Cemetery Hartford with her parents.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Stiles' carries his mother Julia Stiles Hale's maiden surname.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at San Antonio Texas, unmarried, March 1876
narrative_blocks.body: Cornelius Stiles Hale was born July 4, 1839 in Hartford CT — his birthday America's Independence Day — and died March 14, 1876 in San Antonio, Texas at age 36-37, unmarried. The Stiles book records his Texas death but not the circumstances. He was the youngest of Julia Stiles Hale's six children and the last to die — his elder brother Charles Frederick Hale MD outlived him.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909), entry 462
Henry Reed Stiles, The Stiles Family in America (1895), entry [-34] of Job Stiles

career.notes: Shoemaker by trade (EH 462 + Stiles book). The Stiles family resided in Enfield CT for about ten years (c.1810-1821) where Job died in 1813, then his widow returned to Windsor.

burial.plot_notes: Palisado Cemetery, Windsor CT — buried 'on one stone' with wife Mary Drake Stiles.

narrative_blocks.header: Windsor shoemaker, brother of silversmith Samuel
narrative_blocks.body: Job Stiles was a shoemaker by trade, working in Windsor CT and later Enfield CT. His older brother Samuel Stiles was a Northampton silversmith and clockmaker — together they represent two New England craft trades within the same Stiles family generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at forty-eight, buried with Mary in Windsor
narrative_blocks.body: Job died at Windsor CT on April 13, 1813 at age 48, during the family's roughly decade-long Enfield CT residence (c.1810-1821). His widow Mary Drake Stiles survived him by twenty-six years, dying March 14, 1839 at age 77. They were buried together in the Old Windsor Graveyard with their names inscribed on a single stone.

burial.plot_notes: Palisado Cemetery, Windsor CT — 'on one stone' with husband Job Stiles.

parents.father_research_notes: Of Windsor CT. Not in JSON.
parents.mother_research_notes: Of Windsor CT, of the Griswold family — middle name of grandson Ezra Griswold Stiles (HD4158) carries this lineage. Not in JSON.

narrative_blocks.header: Twenty-six years widowed, buried three children
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Drake Stiles was widowed at fifty-one in 1813 when her husband Job died. She lived another twenty-six years in Windsor, dying at seventy-seven in 1839. She buried three of her nine children: infant Mary in September 1786 (age 5 months), Laura in September 1810 (age 21), and Ezra Griswold lost at sea around 1819 (presumed dead at 28).

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909), entry 462
Henry Reed Stiles, The Stiles Family in America (1895)

military_service.notes: Per The Stiles Family in America (BIB008) entry #230: served in the Revolution and was at Horse Neck from May 1781 to March 1782. Horse Neck (Greenwich CT) was an active Revolutionary War outpost under occasional British raid throughout the war.
Served in the Revolution at Horse Neck (now Greenwich CT, on the western frontier facing British-held New York), from May 1781 to March 1782.

narrative_blocks.header: Inherited mansion, lost all endorsing for friend
narrative_blocks.body: Ashbel Stiles inherited the family mansion and a very comfortable property from his father in North Haven Connecticut. But in an evil hour he endorsed for a friend and lost his all. Soon after he removed to Windsor Connecticut, and thence to Norwich Massachusetts — now Huntington — near his daughter Ruth Stiles Ellis.

narrative_blocks.header: Hannah died Norwich one month before Ashbel did
narrative_blocks.body: After fifty-one years of marriage, Ashbel and his cousin-wife Hannah Stiles both died at Norwich (now Huntington) Massachusetts in autumn 1810 — both at age seventy-five. Hannah went first in September; Ashbel followed in October, approximately one month later.

narrative_blocks.header: Son thrown from a horse, killed in Jamaica
narrative_blocks.body: His son Benjamin Stiles died at Jamaica in the West Indies from injuries sustained being thrown from a horse. The Stiles Family in America entry 230 records the death without further circumstance — a son lost an ocean from home to a single fall.

narrative_blocks.header: Horse Neck garrison 1781 after financial ruin
narrative_blocks.body: Forty-six at enlistment, he came to Revolutionary militia service well after most of his cohort. Financial ruin from endorsing for a friend had already emptied the family mansion he had inherited from his father.

parents.father_research_notes: Lt. Samuel Stiles of Windsor CT

bio.notes: Hannah Stiles married her cousin Ashbell Stiles. She was daughter of Lt. Samuel Stiles and Mary Phelps of Windsor CT. Same surname before and after marriage.
